questions.question: ACS can be subdivided into:
questions.explanation: ACS subdivides into UA, STEMI, and NSTEMI. 'UA with ST-depression' is not a separate recognised category. Note D: NSTEMI is defined by absence of ST elevation (not ST depression).

questions.question: Medications useful to prevent and control 'white thrombus' include:

statements.label: D
statements.text: Inhibitors of ADP receptor-mediated platelet aggregation

questions.explanation: White thrombus = platelet thrombus. Anti-platelet agents (aspirin, clopidogrel/ADP inhibitors) target it. Dalteparin (LMWH) targets red thrombus (fibrin/coagulation). Aspirin inhibits (not activates) cyclo-oxygenase.

questions.question: What else should be considered for a patient with suspected ACS in the pre-hospital setting (by non-medical ambulance personnel)?
questions.explanation: Aspirin (160–325 mg chewed) is the key pre-hospital intervention for suspected ACS. IV glucose/insulin, volume loading and paracetamol are not indicated as initial ACS treatments.

questions.question: Which of the following strategies is considered correct in reopening the occluded coronary artery of a patient with STEMI?

statements.label: A
statements.text: Fibrinolytic therapy in all patients followed by PCI in selected cases

statements.label: B
statements.text: Fibrinolytic therapy only if PCI is not possible within the first 90 minutes

statements.label: C
statements.text: According to risk profile, patients with STEMI should be considered for transfer to a PCI-capable centre

statements.label: D
statements.text: If fibrinolytic therapy is given one has to wait at least two hours before conducting PCI

questions.explanation: Primary PCI is preferred if achievable within 90 min; otherwise fibrinolysis. TRANSFER-AMI showed patients should be transferred for PCI immediately after fibrinolysis without waiting. Not all patients need fibrinolysis first.
questions.section: 2.3 Immediate management of ACS / 4.1.1 Restoration of coronary patency

questions.question: Regarding area of infarction, which are the correct statements regarding ECG leads?

statements.label: A
statements.text: Inferior infarction is seen in II, III and aVF

statements.label: B
statements.text: Anterior infarction is seen in V5 and V6

statements.label: C
statements.text: True posterior infarction is characterised by a tall R wave in V1

statements.label: D
statements.text: Septal infarction is seen in I, aVL, V5 and V6

questions.explanation: Inferior = II, III, aVF. Anterior = V2, V3, V4. Lateral = I, aVL, V5, V6. Septal = V1–V3. Posterior = tall R wave V1. V5/V6 are lateral leads, not anterior or septal.

questions.question: Regarding troponins as biomarkers for cardiac injury:

statements.label: A
statements.text: Troponins are equal with CK regarding sensitivity

statements.label: B
statements.text: CK has a better specificity than troponins

statements.label: C
statements.text: Troponins are the first biomarker to rise after myocardial ischaemia

statements.label: D
statements.text: Troponins are elevated for more than 48 hours after myocardial injury

questions.explanation: Troponins are MORE sensitive and MORE specific than CK. They are NOT the first to rise (myoglobin rises earlier). They remain elevated for >48 hours (up to 10–14 days).

questions.question: Which of the following are complications of PCI?
questions.explanation: PCI complications: re-infarction in 2–4% (not 8–10%), ventricular arrhythmias, distal embolisation, and the Bezold–Jarisch reflex causes HYPOtension (not hypertension), especially with RCA reperfusion.

questions.question: Clopidogrel:

statements.label: B
statements.text: Inhibits activation and aggregation of platelets

statements.label: D
statements.text: Is usually given as a 3 to 6 g loading dose

questions.explanation: Clopidogrel blocks the P2Y12 ADP receptor — a different mechanism from aspirin (COX inhibition). Loading dose is 300–600 mg (not 3–6 g).

questions.question: Recognised complications after acute myocardial infarction include:
questions.explanation: Post-MI complications include free wall rupture, VENTRICULAR (not atrial) septal rupture, acute MR, and cardiogenic shock. ASD is not a recognised post-MI complication.

questions.question: A 59-year-old male with STEMI develops hypotension on day one. Cardiogenic shock is confirmed by PA catheter. Regarding further management:

statements.label: A
statements.text: Immediate infusion of crystalloids until MAP stabilises above 90 mmHg

statements.label: B
statements.text: Active treatment of complicating arrhythmias or conduction defects

statements.label: C
statements.text: The use of dobutamine to raise CI towards 3 L/min/m²

statements.label: D
statements.text: Aggressive use of beta-blockers may prevent further instability

questions.explanation: Arrhythmia correction and inotropic support (dobutamine) are appropriate. Aggressive crystalloid loading is not the answer in cardiogenic shock (PAOP already elevated). Beta-blockers may worsen hypotension and are contraindicated in cardiogenic shock.

questions.question: After AMI, PCI is clearly superior to thrombolysis in all of the following EXCEPT:
options: A: Cardiogenic shock
B: Severe heart failure
C: Ventricular arrhythmia-induced haemodynamic instability
D: Contraindications to thrombolytic therapy
E: Patients < 50 years of age
questions.explanation: PCI is clearly superior in shock, severe HF, haemodynamic arrhythmias, and thrombolysis contraindications. Young age alone is not an established indication for PCI superiority over thrombolysis.
questions.section: 4.1.2 Primary angioplasty – Situations where PCI is clearly superior

questions.question: An ECG from a patient with ACS shows ST-segment elevation with tall peaking T waves and progressive upward coving. What is the probable time span since coronary occlusion?
options: A: Minutes to hours (acute)
B: Hours to days (early)
C: Days to weeks (intermediate)
D: Weeks to months (old)
E: Impossible to tell
questions.explanation: Tall peaking T waves with progressive ST elevation (hyperacute → acute phase) occur within minutes to hours of occlusion. Later stages show Q wave development, ST normalisation, and T wave inversion.

questions.question: Important differential diagnoses of ACS include all of the following EXCEPT:
options: A: Lobar pneumonia
B: Aortic dissection
C: Acute pulmonary embolism
D: Acute pericarditis
E: Appendicitis
questions.explanation: Important differentials include aortic dissection, PE, pericarditis, pleuro-pneumonia, gastric ulcer, oesophagitis, and cholecystitis. Appendicitis is not a recognised ACS mimic.

questions.question: Indications to restore coronary patency in AMI include all of the following EXCEPT:
options: A: Successful resuscitation after all cardiac arrests
B: ST-segment elevation > 2 mm in V2–V6
C: Acute posterior infarction
D: New-onset left bundle branch block
E: Continuing pain with evolving infarction 12–24 hours after onset
questions.explanation: Reperfusion is indicated for ST elevation, new LBBB, posterior MI, and evolving infarction 12–24h. Not all cardiac arrest survivors meet criteria — reperfusion requires evidence of STEMI or ongoing ischaemia.

questions.question: A pure right ventricular infarction is likely to present with all of the following EXCEPT:
options: A: Elevation of the jugular venous pressure
B: Hypotension
C: Signs of pulmonary oedema
D: Low or normal pulmonary wedge pressure
E: Hepatic congestion
questions.explanation: RV infarction: elevated JVP, hypotension, clear lungs (no pulmonary oedema), low/normal PAOP, hepatic congestion from RV failure. Pulmonary oedema indicates LV failure, not isolated RV infarction.

questions.question: A 68-year-old ICU patient develops troponin elevation during septic shock with hypotension and tachycardia. According to the universal classification, which MI type is this most likely?
options: A: Type 1 – Spontaneous MI from plaque rupture
B: Type 2 – MI secondary to supply-demand mismatch
C: Type 3 – Sudden cardiac death
D: Type 4a – MI associated with PCI
E: Type 5 – MI associated with CABG
questions.explanation: Type 2 MI is secondary to increased oxygen demand or decreased supply (hypotension, tachycardia, anaemia, sepsis) rather than a primary coronary plaque event. Common in ICU populations.
questions.section: 1.1 Terminology – Table 1: Clinical classification of MI

questions.question: Takotsubo (apical ballooning) syndrome can mimic STEMI. Which finding best distinguishes it from true STEMI?
options: A: Elevated troponin
B: ST-segment elevation on ECG
C: Apical wall motion abnormality on echocardiography
D: Normal coronary arteries on angiography
E: Post-menopausal female patient
questions.explanation: Definitive diagnosis requires angiography showing normal coronary vessels without thrombus. Troponin elevation, ST changes, and apical ballooning on echo can occur in both conditions.

questions.question: Which is an ABSOLUTE contraindication to thrombolytic therapy?
options: A: Severe hypertension 180/110 mmHg
B: Oral anticoagulation with INR > 2.5
C: Active internal bleeding within 2 weeks
D: Major surgery 3 weeks ago
E: Pregnancy
questions.explanation: Active internal bleeding (≤2 weeks, excluding menses) is an absolute contraindication. Severe hypertension, anticoagulation, recent surgery and pregnancy are relative contraindications.

questions.question: What is the primary mechanism of IABP counterpulsation?
options: A: Direct myocardial contractility enhancement
B: Reduction of systolic afterload and augmentation of diastolic coronary perfusion
C: Volume redistribution from pulmonary to systemic circulation
D: Increased heart rate to improve cardiac output
E: Direct fibrinolysis of coronary thrombus
questions.explanation: IABP deflates in systole (reducing afterload) and inflates in diastole (augmenting coronary perfusion pressure), improving cardiac output and coronary flow without increasing oxygen demand.

questions.question: A post-MI patient develops a new pansystolic murmur, parasternal thrill, and heart failure. PA catheter shows oxygen step-up at the RV level. What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: A: Acute MR from papillary muscle rupture
B: Ventricular septal rupture
C: Free wall rupture with tamponade
D: Acute aortic regurgitation
E: RV infarction
questions.explanation: Oxygen step-up from RA to RV is the hallmark of a left-to-right shunt through a ventricular septal rupture. Both VSD and acute MR produce V waves, but only VSD causes the step-up.

questions.question: Ventricular free wall rupture typically occurs in which patient profile?
options: A: Young male with family history of CAD
B: Elderly female with hypertension and first infarction
C: Middle-aged male with prior CABG
D: Diabetic patient with recurrent NSTEMI
E: Young patient with cocaine-induced MI
questions.explanation: Classic profile: elderly female, chronic hypertension, first infarction. Bimodal timing: peaks at ~24 hours and 3–5 days. Early reperfusion reduces risk, but late thrombolysis (>14h) may increase it.

questions.question: Which haemodynamic parameters define cardiogenic shock?
options: A: SBP <90 mmHg (≥30 min), CI <2.2 L/min/m², PAOP >15–18 mmHg
B: SBP <100 mmHg, CI <3.0 L/min/m², PAOP >12 mmHg
C: MAP <65 mmHg, CI <2.5 L/min/m², PAOP <12 mmHg
D: SBP <80 mmHg, CI <1.5 L/min/m², CVP <5 mmHg
E: SBP <90 mmHg, CI <2.2 L/min/m², PAOP <10 mmHg
questions.explanation: Cardiogenic shock: sustained SBP <90 mmHg ≥30 min, CI <2.2 L/min/m², and elevated PAOP >15–18 mmHg. The elevated PAOP distinguishes it from hypovolaemic shock.

questions.question: In the GUSTO trial, accelerated tPA compared to streptokinase resulted in:
options: A: No difference in 30-day mortality
B: 14% relative reduction in 30-day mortality (6.3% vs 7.4%), saving ~10 additional lives per 1000
C: 30% relative reduction in mortality but unacceptable bleeding
D: Superior outcomes only in patients under 60 years
E: Lower rate of intracranial haemorrhage
questions.explanation: GUSTO showed tPA 6.3% vs SK 7.4% 30-day mortality (14% relative reduction, ~10 lives/1000). tPA had slightly HIGHER intracranial haemorrhage risk, particularly in patients >75 years.

questions.question: Which of the following is a theoretical advantage of LMWH over unfractionated heparin?
options: A: Shorter half-life allowing easier titration
B: Requires aPTT monitoring for dose adjustment
C: Predictable pharmacokinetics without need for aPTT monitoring
D: Superior efficacy in patients with renal failure
E: Can be fully reversed with protamine
questions.explanation: LMWH advantages: predictable pharmacokinetics (no aPTT monitoring needed), enhanced anti-Xa activity, subcutaneous administration, and lower incidence of HIT. LMWH has a LONGER half-life than UFH and is less suitable in renal failure.
questions.section: 4.1.4 Therapies – UFH, LMWH and Direct Thrombin Inhibitors

questions.question: Regarding secondary prevention after MI, which statement about ACE inhibitors is correct?
options: A: ACE inhibitors should only be started if blood pressure is elevated
B: Benefit is limited to patients with normal EF
C: Patients with anterior MI and EF <40% may experience a 20% relative mortality reduction
D: ARBs have no role as alternatives to ACE inhibitors
E: ACE inhibitors should be started immediately in cardiogenic shock
questions.explanation: SAVE, HOPE, and EUROPA showed that ACE inhibitors reduce mortality in high-risk patients, especially with anterior MI and EF <40% (~20% relative reduction). Candesartan and valsartan are effective ARB alternatives. ACE inhibitors should be withheld in cardiogenic shock until stable.
questions.section: 6.1.4 ACE inhibitors and angiotensin receptor blockers

questions.question: Development of new bundle branch block during acute anterior MI indicates:
options: A: Benign conduction disturbance requiring observation only
B: Distal LAD occlusion with small infarct size
C: Proximal LAD occlusion with extensive anterior wall infarction
D: Right coronary artery occlusion
E: Pericarditis complicating the infarction
questions.explanation: New BBB during anterior MI indicates proximal LAD occlusion with extensive infarction. Early death from pump failure and VT/VF is common. Aggressive treatment with primary PCI is required, and temporary pacing may be needed for 2nd/3rd degree block.

questions.question: A patient with acute MI has persistent pulmonary congestion despite restored tissue perfusion. Blood pressure is 105/70 mmHg. Which agent is most appropriate to reduce preload?
options: A: Dobutamine
B: Norepinephrine
C: Nitroglycerin
D: Dopamine
E: Milrinone
questions.explanation: Nitroglycerin is an effective venodilator that reduces PAOP and can decrease ischaemia by redistributing coronary flow. It is appropriate when blood pressure permits. Sodium nitroprusside is a balanced vasodilator alternative. Both require careful titration.
questions.section: 5.9.6 Managing patients with adequate perfusion but pulmonary congestion
